ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Singapore Laksa specifically; mapped to broader noodle dish class. Boston Lobster variant is a premium restaurant adaptation with no dedicated ontology entry.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish — soup noodles with seafood
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singapore Laksa or Boston Lobster Laksa. A0CKP (soup noodles with seafood) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Singapore Laksa or this dish variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Boston Lobster Singapore Laksa weighs approximately 900 g and includes a whole Boston lobster, a generous portion of rice vermicelli, coconut-based laksa gravy, fish cake, tau pok, bean sprouts, and laksa leaf garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Laksa (Singapore-style, coconut milk base) and USDA FoodData Central — Lobster, cooked (SR Legacy #15147)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and secondary nutrients for the laksa base (coconut milk gravy, rice vermicelli, fish cake, tau pok, bean sprouts) are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo laksa entries. Lobster protein, fat, and cholesterol contributions are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #15147 (lobster, cooked). The input per_100g values (energy 138.89 kcal, protein 8.33 g, fat 9.44 g, carbohydrate 6.11 g) are consistent with a weighted composite of these two approved sources for a 900 g bowl and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are not reported in the HPB Foodo laksa entries and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli / rice noodles (GI ~58–61, glucose reference); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for Boston Lobster Singapore Laksa as a composite dish. GI of 58 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source — rice vermicelli (bee hoon) — which has a published GI of approximately 58–61 (glucose reference) per Atkinson et al. 2008. The coconut milk gravy and protein from lobster and fish cake are expected to moderately attenuate the overall GI relative to plain rice vermicelli. GL per serving calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving in grams (900 g × 6.11 g/100g = 54.99 g) / 100 = 31.9, rounded to 32.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from belacan, fish cake, and laksa paste; low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Boston Lobster Singapore Laksa to small portions or avoid it entirely, as sodium at 311 mg/100g (approximately 2,800 mg per 900 g bowl) substantially exceeds the 2,000 mg/day recommended limit, and the absence of meaningful potassium or fibre offset further elevates cardiovascular risk; requesting reduced belacan and fish cake and consuming less than half the gravy can partially mitigate sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-GI rice vermicelli as dominant carbohydrate; high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that while per-100g carbohydrate is modest at 6.11 g, a full 900 g bowl delivers approximately 55 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~32 — a high GL per meal — and should request a reduced noodle portion, substitute with lower-GI options such as konjac noodles, and account for this dish within their total daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.11 g/100g from coconut milk; cholesterol 38.89 mg/100g; high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Boston Lobster Singapore Laksa sparingly, as the high saturated fat content (6.11 g/100g, approximately 55 g per bowl) from coconut milk promotes LDL elevation and arterial inflammation, the sodium load (~2,800 mg per bowl) contributes to hypertensive arterial stress, and the cholesterol from lobster and fish cake (~350 mg per bowl) approaches or exceeds the daily threshold for individuals with established CVD; requesting reduced coconut milk and smaller portions is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat exceeds 5 g/100g RA threshold; AGEs from tau pok deep-frying
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish, as saturated fat at 6.11 g/100g from coconut milk exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold, tau pok (fried tofu puffs) introduces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that amplify TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity, and chilli in the laksa paste is a nightshade that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boston Lobster Singapore Laksa is a premium restaurant-grade rendition of the classic Singaporean laksa, featuring a whole Boston lobster (Homarus americanus) as the centrepiece, served in a rich, spicy, and aromatic coconut milk-based gravy over thick rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The dish is a signature offering in Singaporean zi char restaurants and seafood establishments, combining the luxurious sweetness of fresh lobster meat with the bold, complex flavours of traditional laksa paste made from dried shrimp, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, and dried chillies. The gravy is simmered with coconut milk to produce a creamy, orange-hued broth that is simultaneously spicy, savoury, and slightly sweet. Traditional garnishes include fish cake slices, tau pok (fried tofu puffs), bean sprouts, and fresh laksa leaves (Vietnamese coriander / daun kesum). Nutritionally, the dish provides 138.89 kcal per 100 g, with protein at 8.33 g/100g from lobster and fish cake, fat at 9.44 g/100g predominantly from coconut milk (saturated fat 6.11 g/100g), and carbohydrate at 6.11 g/100g from rice vermicelli. Sodium is elevated at 311 mg/100g due to belacan and fish cake, making this dish a high-sodium meal at restaurant serving sizes (~2,800 mg per 900 g bowl). The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the rice vermicelli component, with a high glycemic load of approximately 32 per serving. Clinically, this dish poses moderate-to-high concern for patients with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat and sodium profile, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the high per-meal glycemic load. It is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not certified halal due to the use of belacan. The dish is culturally significant as a celebration or special-occasion meal in Singapore's Peranakan and zi char culinary traditions.

What is the GI of laksa?

Is Boston Lobster Laksa suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros are consistent with a weighted composite of HPB Singapore Foodo laksa entries and USDA FoodData Central lobster data, but no single approved source entry exists for Boston Lobster Singapore Laksa as a named dish. Values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalents. GI is an analogous estimate from rice vermicelli data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are unavailable in the referenced approved sources for this dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Singapore Laksa (coconut milk base with lobster) is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for Singapore Laksa is not available in HPB Foodo entries consulted. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo laksa entries or USDA SR Legacy lobster entries for this composite dish. 5. The input source listed (Malaysian Food Composition Database / MyFCD) was not used as the primary nutrient source because this dish is Singaporean in origin and HPB Singapore Foodo is the higher-priority approved source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. MyFCD is retained as a supporting reference for regional context only. 6. Halal status is marked false because traditional laksa paste contains belacan (fermented shrimp paste), which may not be certified halal; individual restaurant preparation may vary. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.06 g/100g is retained from the input; this is a trace level consistent with small amounts of partially hydrogenated oils potentially present in commercially produced fish cake or tau pok.
